scenes.content: Everything you see on your screen right now, are just numbers. Games, Videos and even the frame you are watching right now is not English, not images, just numbers. And today we are going to decode how computers understand these numbers and perform various operations.
scenes.title: Hook

scenes.content:
Hi everyone, welcome back ! My name is Arkadyuti and this is the first episode of the class 11 computer science series. By the end of this video, you’ll understand:

What number systems are
Binary, Octal, Decimal, Hexadecimal number systems
And how to convert between them like a pro”

So lets start.
scenes.title: Intro

scenes.content:
Imagine a number… say 729. What does this actually mean ? 

It means, there are 7 hundreds, 2 tens and 9 ones. so basically the number can be written as 7 × 100 + 2 × 10 + 9 × 1.
And if we go one step deeper,
100 is 10²,
10 is 10¹,
and 1 is 10⁰.

So now the same number can be expanded as:
7 × 10² + 2 × 10¹ + 9 × 10⁰ = 729.

Notice something interesting ? 

As we move from right to left,
the power of 10 keeps increasing.
But you may ask
Why base 10?
Why not 9… or 11?
The reason is simple.
In the decimal number system,
we only have 10 digits:
0 to 9.
This number is also called as the base of a number system...which is 10 in case of decimal.

Various number systems have various bases. For example binary has base 2, octal has base 8 and hexadecimal has base 16. Note that the base of a number system also denotes how many digits the number system has in total.
scenes.title: What is a number System ?

scenes.content:
Now let us study the binary system in detail. 
Unlike decimal, which uses 10 digits, binary uses only two:
0 and 1.
That’s it. No other digits, just 0 and 1. So you might be wondering how can just two digits represent everything? Well that question of yours's will be answered in the next video, logic gates and computer systems. But for now, don't let curiosity kill the cat.

Anyways moving ahead, 
Now just like decimal uses powers of 10, binary uses powers of 2. To understand, lets take an example, say 1011, just like decimal, here too each number will get multiplied with a power of 2, starting from zero and increasing as we go left.  So
1011 can be expanded as 1 × 2⁰ + 1 × 2¹ + 0 × 2² + 1 × 2³
 and solving further, we get the value as 11. So in simple words, 11 is the decimal representation of binary 1011 and this is the process by which we convert a binary number into decimal. 

Let's take another example, say 10010.........(do the same thing again)
Now converting a decimal number into binary is a pretty easy one. You just keep dividing the number by the base, which is 2, and record the remainder in each division step until you cannot divide anymore. 
After you are done, arrange all the remainders in the reverse order, starting from the last one to the first one. Congrats, you just converted a binary number to decimal.
scenes.title: Binary
Octal
scenes.content:
Next on our list is octal number system. As the name suggests, octal number system has 8 digits in total, starting from 0 all the up to 7. So, the base in this case will be 8. Conversion between decimal and octal is exactly same as we had just done in binary, except just use 8 instead of 2. 

For example to convert 237 in octal to decimal, we write the following expanded form using powers of 8 as previously done in binary and decimal. and then add all the terms together to obtain our decimal version of that number which is 159.

Interestingly, converting decimal to octal is also exactly same as binary, just that we need to use 8 instead of 2....then keep dividing until we cannot, and then collect the reminders in the reverse order of their arrival.

But there is an interesting caveat here. By this point we know that each place in octal system can be represented as a multiple of the power of 8. and 8 is 2 cube. this means one octal digit can represent a 3 digit binary number. Lets try to visualize this :
Let’s take:
110101

Now group from right:

(110)(101)

Now convert each group:

110 = 6
101 = 5

So:
👉 110101 = 65 (octal)

This way you can convert binary to octal directly. It is very natural to not understand this part on the first go, please consider rewinding and watching it a second time.

scenes.title: Hexadecimal
scenes.content:
Now let's proceed to Hexadecimal number system. And as you expect, hexadecimal number system has 16 numbers, ranging from 0 to 9 and because there are no more digits, we use additional 6 alphabets A to F. Now here A here means 10, B means 11 and so on and so forth until F which is 15. 

Inter-converting between hexadecimal and decimal is also similar to how we did it previously for binary and octal.


Just expand using powers of 16 instead.

Let’s say 4C.

Now, just like before, we assign powers from right to left:

4 × 16¹ + C × 16⁰

But remember,
C represents 12.

So this becomes:

4 × 16 + 12 × 1
= 64 + 12
= 76

So,
👉 4C in hexadecimal is equal to 76 in decimal.

Now let’s go the other way.

Converting decimal to hexadecimal follows the exact same pattern we’ve already seen.

We simply divide by 16 and keep track of remainders.

Let’s take 76 again:

76 ÷ 16 = 4 remainder 12

Now 12 is represented as C.

So the answer becomes:
👉 4C

Now here comes the interesting part…

Just like we grouped binary digits in sets of 3 for octal,
we group them in sets of 4 for hexadecimal.

Why 4?

Because:
2⁴ = 16

So one hexadecimal digit can represent exactly 4 binary digits.

Take the binary number:
11001100

Group from right:

(1100)(1100)

Now convert each group:

1100 = 12 = C
1100 = 12 = C

So:
👉 11001100 = CC (hexadecimal)

scenes.title: Conclusion
scenes.content: If you liked this video then please consider liking and subscribing, also comment down below if you want a dedicated video on problem solving related to this chapter, what I could improve.

scenes.title: Outro
scenes.content:
“No matter which number system we use…

the rules never really change.

We’re always:

Expanding using powers of the base
Or reducing using division by the base

Different systems…
same logic… just a different lens.
